title: A CCURATE AND FAST DETECTION OF COPY NUM - BER VARIATIONS FROM SHORT - READ WHOLE - GENOME SEQUENCING WITH DEEP CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORK

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

- ACCURATE AND FAST DETECTION OF COPY NUM - - BER VARIATIONS FROM SHORT READ WHOLE GENOME SEQUENCING WITH DEEP CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORK

**Anonymous authors** Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

A copy number variant (CNV) is a type of genetic mutation where a stretch of DNA is lost or duplicated once or multiple times. CNVs play important roles in the development of diseases and complex traits. CNV detection with short-read DNA sequencing technology is challenging because CNVs significantly vary in size and are similar to DNA sequencing artifacts. Many methods have been developed but still yield unsatisfactory results with high computational costs. Here, we propose CNV-Net, a novel approach for CNV detection using a six-layer convolutional neural network. We encode DNA sequencing information into RGB images and train the convolutional neural network with these images. The fitted convolutional neural network can then be used to predict CNVs from DNA sequencing data. We benchmark CNV-Net with two high-quality whole-genome sequencing datasets available from the Genome in a Bottle Consortium, considered as gold standard benchmarking datasets for CNV detection. We demonstrate that CNV-Net is more

## 1 INTRODUCTION

A copy number variant (CNV) is a genetic mutation where a stretch of DNA is completely lost or repeated more than once compared to a reference genome. CNV sizes range from 50 bases to 3 million bases or more with two major types: duplication if the DNA sequence is repeated and deletion if a DNA sequence is missing. CNVs are spread along a genome, which account for 4.8% to 9.5% of human genome (Zarrei et al., 2015). They are known to influence many complex diseases including autism (Sebat et al., 2007), bipolar disorder (Green et al., 2016), schizophrenia (Stefansson et al., 2008), and Alzheimer’ disease (Cuccaro et al., 2017) as well as gene expression (Chiang et al., 2017). As CNVs may overlap with a large portion of a genome, such as one gene or even multiple genes, their effect on disease may be substantially larger than that of single nucleotide variants, and hence CNVs often play important roles in the genetic mechanisms of diseases and complex traits.

With the advent of next-generation DNA sequencing technologies over the past decade, the resolution and scale of CNV detection has been greatly improved as large-scale sequencing studies become feasible. However, CNVs are still challenging to detect from short-read next-generation DNA sequencing techniques due to the significantly varying sizes of CNVs and their similarity with common DNA sequencing artifacts. Many computational methods have been developed for the discovery of CNVs from short-read DNA sequencing data, but their performance is often unsatisfactory due to low accuracy and high computational cost (Kosugi et al., 2019). The main reason for this is that previous methods (Rausch et al., 2012; Abyzov et al., 2011) mainly rely on the statistical analysis of the signals from read alignments (a process that maps reads from DNA sequencing data to the reference genome), which often fail to employ all useful features of DNA sequencing data and require significant computational resources. Therefore, there is a need for a novel, sophisticated computational tool to improve CNV detection with higher accuracy and efficiency.
Here, we present CNV-Net, a new approach to identify CNVs from DNA sequencing data using a six-layer deep convolutional neural network (CNN). CNV-Net first encodes the reads and their

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

related information from DNA sequencing data such as the type of base, base coverage (the number of reads covering a position), and base quality of each position into the RGB channels of images. Then it uses a deep CNN to predict these images as deletions, duplications, or false positives (not a CNV) breakpoints. Note that a CNV is defined by two breakpoints, that is, its start and end positions. Although it has been shown that CNNs such as DeepVariant (Poplin et al., 2018) and Clairvoyante (Luo et al., 2019) can accurately detect single nucleotide variants from similarly encoded images or tensors, few CNNs have been designed for the identification of CNVs. The key advantage of CNVNet over other methods is the application of deep learning, which allows learning features with higher complexity from DNA sequencing data. We train the network with the Genome in a Bottle Consortium NA12878 dataset (Pendleton et al., 2015), and demonstrate CNV-Net performance on a high-quality benchmarking dataset acquired from a well-studied cell line, HG002 (Zook et al., 2020). We also compare its performance to that of previous methods.

## 2 METHODS

CNV-Net first encodes sequencing information into pileup images and classifies each image as a deletion, duplication, or false positive (not a CNV). In this section, we describe how we generate pileup images, that is, the input to CNV-Net, as well as the design of the CNN.

## 2.1 PILEUP IMAGE GENERATION

We convert the information of mapped reads from DNA sequencing data (provided in BAM file format) and reference genome information to 201 _×_ 55 pixel image representation of 201-base wide regions. Training data pileup images contain a CNV breakpoint (start or end position) centered in the image, capturing the 100 positions left and right each CNV breakpoint. Using the RGB channels of an image, this snapshot is able to capture features of the reads at specific locations in the reference genome.

Figure 1: 201 _×_ 55 pixel pileup image. Feature rows are labeled by brackets and RGB channels are labeled by curly braces. Reference rows are encoded in R channel by base A: 250, G: 180, T: 100, C: 30, N: 0. Coverage rows are encoded in RG channels by the number of mapped reads covering each position. Reads are encoded in RGB channels by base types, base quality, and strand directionality positive: 70, negative: 240.

As shown in Figure 1, the first five rows of the image capture the reference genome across a region of 201 base pairs by encoding the R channel of each pixel with an individual base in that position: A’s as 250, G’s as 180, T’s as 100, C’s as 30, and N’s as 0. Under the five rows representing the reference genome are another five rows that capture the read depth (coverage) at that position. The R and G channels are encoded with an adjusted read depth value between 0 and 255. The following 45 rows capture the mapped reads from DNA sequencing. The R channel encodes for the nucleotide, which is the same as the reference genome, and the G channel encodes for the base quality found in the BAM file, which is adjusted to be between 0 and 255. Lastly, the B channel encodes for strand directionality: 70 if positive and 240 if negative.

Each 201 _×_ 55 pixel pileup image, thus, captures coverage and reads specific to the 201-base region of the reference genome such that the sequencing data may be re-created from a series of consecutive pileup images. If there are more than 45 reads mapping to a specific region, those reads are discarded; we instead compensate by capturing this information through the rows that encodes coverage. If no reads map to the region, the image is left blank below the first five rows for the reference genome.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## 2.2 DESIGN OF CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORK

Figure 2: CNV-Net architecture. CNV-Net employs a six-layer convolutional neural network for detecting copy number variations from DNA sequencing data. Descriptions under each layer indicate: type of layer, dimension of layer in parenthesis (input layer: height _×_ width _×_ channels, convolutional layer: height _×_ width _×_ filters, and fully connected layer: nodes), kernel size in brackets. The model predicts the probability of deletion (“DEL”), duplication (“DUP”), and false positive (“FP”) breakpoint presence in each input pileup image.

As presented in Figure 2, CNV-Net consists of four convolutional layers and two fully connected layers with a total of 5,110,979 parameters. Each of the four convolutional layers uses 3 _×_ 3 kernels and the RELU activation function. Each convolutional layer is followed by batch normalization, average pooling, and 0.4 dropout. Arrays are flattened in between the four convolutional layers and two fully connected layers followed by softmax output to predictions for CNV breakpoint presence in the 201-base window: duplication, deletion, or false positive (not a CNV).

In training CNV-net, we use a standard Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba, 2014) with a sparse categorical cross-entropy loss function. We use a cyclical learning rate initialized at 1e-5 and maximal 1e-2. To reduce overfitting, we train the model until the accuracy cannot be improved in 15 epochs. The 201-base regions are centered around the CNV breakpoints, that is, the starts and ends of CNVs in the training set capturing the 100 positions directly before and after each CNV breakpoint. False positive images include random 201-base regions outside of CNVs. From the NA12878 dataset, a total of 52,243 pileup images are labeled as duplications, deletions, and false positives from the training set randomly split into 70%, 20%, 10% categories for training set, validation set, and test set for the network, respectively.

## 3 RESULTS

In this study, we use two standard structural variation benchmarking datasets: NA12878 and HG002. Both these benchmarking datasets are mapped to the GRCh37 reference genome. NA12878 is a well-studied genome provided by the Genome in a Bottle Consortium. It is whole-genome sequenced with 50 _×_ coverage and each read in the sequencing data is 101 bases long. From the NA12878 image dataset (Pendleton et al., 2015), we generate images for 22,936 duplications, 18,614 deletions, and 10,693 false positives (not CNVs) breakpoints. The HG002 dataset (Zook et al., 2020) is another robust benchmarking dataset derived by the Genome in a Bottle Consortium, which is sequenced with 60 _×_ coverage. Reads in the HG002 dataset are all 148 bases long. As we only consider CNVs passing the quality control filter, this dataset contains 174 duplications, 10,668 deletions, and 5,205 false positives breakpoints post-filtering.

We train the convolutional neural network on the NA12878 dataset and test the fitted model on the HG002 dataset. We also compare CNV-Net with popular existing tools for CNV detection, including BreakDancer (Chen et al., 2009), CNVnator (Abyzov et al., 2011), Delly (Rausch et al.,

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

2012), Lumpy (Layer et al., 2014), and Manta (Chen et al., 2016). It is important to note that CNVNet makes predictions using the pileup images of CNVs where their start and end positions are given, and hence we assume that candidate CNV positions are given as input to CNV-Net. However, other methods find the candidate CNV positions using the information on how reads are mapped to the reference genome and detect CNVs on those candidate CNV positions.

The NA12878 dataset is preprocessed and converted into RGB images as described in the Methods section. We split the CNVs in NA12878 into 70%, 20%, 10% for training set, validation set, and test set, respectively. We train the CNV-Net with the training set and report its performance metrics on the test set. As shown in Table 1, CNV-Net identifies duplications with F1-score of 0.7833, deletions with F1-score of 0.7473, and false positives with F1-score of 0.7124. This result shows that CNV-Net achieves high accuracy in the test set of the NA12878 dataset.

Table 1: Performance of CNV-Net on the test set of the NA12878 dataset

|**CNV types**|**Precision**|**Recall**|**F1-score**|
|---|---|---|---|
|Duplications|0.8376|0.7356|0.7833|
|Deletions|0.7885|0.7102|0.7473|
|Falsepositives|0.6046|0.8672|0.7124|

Next, we evaluate our fitted CNV-Net on the HG002 dataset and compare it with five existing tools for CNV detection. This training scheme represents the real data analysis as we will train CNVNet with CNVs from known genomes and testing it on different genomes. As demonstrated in Table 2, CNV-Net achieves the highest F1-score in CNV detection in the HG002 dataset among all six methods. It also has the highest recall and the second highest precision. This result shows that CNV-Net is more accurate in CNV detection than current methods.

We then compare the time cost of applying different methods to the HG002 dataset. CNV-Net is significantly faster than other methods (Table 3). That is because a fitted convolutional neural network is efficient in making predictions, however, it may be time-consuming during model training. Run time is effectively front-loaded into model training instead of a concurrent statistical analysis. Another reason for this can be that CNV-Net makes predictions on candidate CNVs so it does not need to spend time searching for candidate CNVs in DNA sequencing data as if other methods do.

Table 3: Runtime of CNV-Net and other methods on the HG002 dataset

|**Tools**|**Runtime**|
|---|---|
|BreakDancer|92 minutes|
|CNVnator|51 minutes|
|Delly|565 minutes|
|Lumpy|46 minutes|
|Manta|36 minutes|
|**CNV-Net**|**16.62 seconds**|

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## 4 CONCLUSION

CNV-Net encodes DNA sequencing information into RGB images and applies a deep convolutional neural network to learn complex features from DNA sequencing data to discover CNVs. We demonstrate that CNV-Net can detect CNVs breakpoints from DNA sequencing data with significantly higher accuracy and efficiency than existing tools.

To the best of our knowledge, CNV-Net is the first tool to use a CNN to detect CNVs from DNA sequencing data and achieve high performance on the standard benchmarks. It changes the problem of CNV detection from a process of statistical modeling requiring professional expertise into a process of optimizing a general deep learning model. Besides, although we benchmark CNV-Net using only human samples in this study, it can also be applied to DNA sequencing data of other species.

One limitation of CNV-Net is its need to know candidate CNV positions, potential start and end positions of CNVs, before applying the CNN. This is because CNV-Net makes predictions on pileup images generated at the start and end positions of candidate CNVs. In our analysis, we used candidate CNV positions in human genomes, which were previously identified by the Genome in a Bottle Consortium using a suite of other algorithms. In genome sequencing datasets where CNVs have not been analyzed, these candidate CNV positions may not be available. One solution to this problem may be using candidate CNV positions detected by other CNV detection algorithms, especially algorithms with high recall, as CNV-Net can remove false positive CNVs from those CNV positions. Additionally, we may also develop a sensitive candidate CNV detection algorithm, which may have low precision but high recall. Our future research direction involves identifying candidate CNV positions from genome sequencing datasets and incorporating them with CNV-Net; nevertheless, CNV-Net demonstrates potential as a valuable and effective tool for CNV detection.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces CNV-Net, a six-layer CNN architecture designed to classify pre-identified genomic windows as deletions, duplications, or false positives using pileup images. While the model demonstrates improvements in runtime and accuracy compared to several baseline tools on the HG002 dataset, it relies on an assumption that candidate breakpoints are already provided, which limits its utility as a standalone CNV caller.

The paper provides a novel image-based encoding strategy for genomic data that effectively reduces computational overhead compared to traditional statistical callers. However, the reliance on pre-defined candidate regions and the lack of a robust evaluation regarding genomic bias significantly weaken the claim of being a general-purpose CNV detection tool.

Overview issues:

- Circular dependency on existing CNV callers: The methodology in Section 2.2 and the conclusion acknowledge that CNV-Net requires candidate CNV positions as input. This creates a reliance on traditional tools to generate these candidates, meaning the reported speed improvements ignore the runtime of the upstream caller needed to identify the regions in the first place. Without an integrated candidate generation module, the tool cannot function on raw sequencing data. The authors should either implement a high-recall, low-precision sliding window candidate generator or explicitly benchmark the total runtime including the upstream tool required to supply the input coordinates.

- Inadequate handling of genomic biases: The pileup image generation described in Section 2.1 encodes coverage and base quality but ignores systematic biases such as GC content, mappability scores, and repetitive sequence artifacts. These factors are known to cause significant false positives in short-read WGS. By failing to account for these, the model risks over-fitting to the specific noise profile of the training dataset (NA12878). I suggest adding an analysis of performance across different GC-content strata or incorporating mappability tracks as an additional channel in the input image.

- Limited generalizability across sequencing depths: The model is trained on 50x coverage data and tested on 60x coverage data. While this shows some adaptability, the paper does not demonstrate how performance degrades at lower coverage (e.g., 10x-30x), which is common in clinical practice. The reliance on raw read counts in the pileup images (Section 2.1) is particularly sensitive to depth variations. A more robust evaluation would involve downsampling the HG002 dataset to various depths to establish the lower bound of the model's utility.

- Incomplete benchmarking against modern state-of-the-art: The comparison in Section 3 includes older tools like BreakDancer and CNVnator but omits more recent, high-performance callers such as Manta (which is included but often outperformed by newer methods) or ensemble approaches. Furthermore, the metrics provided in Table 2 are heavily influenced by the fact that CNV-Net is given the truth-set candidates. Comparing a supervised classifier that knows the candidate positions against unsupervised callers that must search the entire genome is an apples-to-oranges comparison. The authors should clarify this distinction in the results section and ideally compare against a baseline that uses the same candidate regions.

- Lack of sensitivity analysis for the 201-base window size: The choice of a 201-base window for the input pileup image is a central design parameter, yet the paper provides no justification for this scale. Since CNVs range from 50 bases to over 3 million, a fixed 201-base window may be insufficient for capturing complex structural variations or large duplications. The paper should include an analysis of how detection performance varies with window size, perhaps by testing a few alternative widths (e.g., 500 or 1000 bases) to demonstrate that the model is not overly sensitive to this hyperparameter. This is necessary to understand if the model's accuracy is robust across the actual size distribution of clinical CNVs.

- Missing simulation study to validate the CNN's feature learning: The paper claims that the CNN learns higher complexity features than traditional statistical methods, but it does not isolate these features to demonstrate their utility. A standard way to validate this would be to generate synthetic data with known, controlled artifacts—such as varying GC content or specific read-depth noise profiles—and show that the CNN successfully classifies these regions while traditional statistical methods (like CNVnator) fail. Without such a controlled experiment, it is difficult to distinguish whether the performance gains are due to superior feature learning or simply the model's ability to memorize the specific noise characteristics of the NA12878 dataset.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Implement and evaluate a simple, high-recall candidate generation module so the tool can function as a standalone pipeline.

- Perform a sensitivity analysis by downsampling the test data to lower coverage levels (10x, 20x, 30x) to define the model's operational range.

- Add a discussion or an input channel accounting for GC content and mappability to demonstrate resilience against known sequencing artifacts.

- Revise the performance comparison to explicitly state that the baseline tools were not provided the same candidate regions, or re-run the baselines using the same candidate input to ensure a fair comparison.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in F1-score calculation
comments.explanation: The F1-score for BreakDancer in Table 2 is mathematically inconsistent with the provided precision and recall values. Using the standard formula F1 = 2 * (Precision * Recall) / (Precision + Recall), the result for these inputs is approximately 0.1003, not 0.05923. Please recompute the F1-scores for all entries in Table 2 to ensure they align with the reported metrics.

comments.title: Misleading runtime and accuracy comparisons
comments.quote: Another reason for this can be that CNV-Net makes predictions on candidate CNVs so it does not need to spend time searching for candidate CNVs in DNA sequencing data as if other methods do.
comments.explanation: The comparisons in Tables 2 and 3 are fundamentally biased because they contrast a supervised classifier restricted to pre-identified candidate regions against unsupervised callers performing genome-wide discovery. The reported runtime ignores the computational cost of the upstream candidate generation, and the performance metrics reflect a classification task rather than de novo detection. Please clarify that the baseline tools are being evaluated on their ability to perform full discovery while CNV-Net is restricted to classification, and provide a runtime comparison that includes the necessary candidate generation step.

comments.title: Saturation of coverage encoding
comments.quote: If there are more than 45 reads mapping to a specific region, those reads are discarded; we instead compensate by capturing this information through the rows that encodes coverage.
comments.explanation: The claim that coverage rows compensate for discarded reads is underspecified. The coverage section uses 5 rows of 201 pixels, but the mapping to the 0-255 range is not defined. If the read depth at a position exceeds 255, the encoding saturates, leading to irreversible information loss. Please define the specific normalization function used to map raw depth to the 0-255 range and explicitly state the maximum depth the model can distinguish before saturation occurs.

comments.title: Ambiguous architecture description
comments.quote: Arrays are flattened in between the four convolutional layers and two fully connected layers followed by softmax output
comments.explanation: The description of the flattening operation is ambiguous and potentially implies a non-standard architecture. If flattening occurs between every convolutional layer, the spatial dimensions would be lost, preventing subsequent convolutions. Please clarify that flattening occurs only once, after the final convolutional layer, to accurately reflect the model's structure.

comments.title: Unverified parameter count
comments.quote: As presented in Figure 2, CNV-Net consists of four convolutional layers and two fully connected layers with a total of 5,110,979 parameters.
comments.explanation: The provided parameter count cannot be verified without the specific number of filters per convolutional layer and the number of nodes in the fully connected layers. Please include a table detailing the architecture dimensions to justify this total.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces CNV-Net, a six-layer CNN that classifies 201-base pileup images as deletions, duplications, or false positives. While the image-based encoding strategy is intuitive, the experimental design contains methodological flaws that compromise the validity of the performance claims. Data leakage from sample-level splitting, misleading runtime comparisons, and the absence of bias correction or size-stratified evaluation significantly weaken the reported results.

The paper tackles a relevant problem in computational genomics by applying deep learning to CNV breakpoint classification. The image encoding pipeline is straightforward, and the architecture is simple enough to reproduce. However, the current evaluation framework overstates the model's generalization capability and practical utility. Addressing the data splitting strategy, benchmarking methodology, and biological context would substantially improve the paper's scientific rigor.

Overview issues:

- Sample-level data splitting introduces severe leakage: The authors split CNV calls from the single NA12878 genome into 70/20/10 training, validation, and test sets. Because all splits originate from the same sequencing run, the underlying read distributions, coverage biases, and instrument artifacts are identical across splits. The CNN will inevitably memorize sample-specific noise rather than learning transferable CNV signatures, inflating test performance. Standard practice in genomic deep learning requires strict sample-level separation to prevent this exact leakage. The results in Table 1 therefore overstate generalization capability. The authors must retrain using independent genomes or explicitly frame the current setup as a proof-of-concept rather than a benchmark.

- Runtime comparison conflates inference with full detection: Table 3 reports CNV-Net runtime as 16.62 seconds, yet the authors explicitly state the model requires candidate CNV positions as input. All competing tools perform both candidate generation and classification in the reported times. By measuring only the CNN inference step, the speed advantage is artificially inflated. This framing misleads readers about the actual computational burden of deploying CNV-Net in a real workflow. The authors should either report the total pipeline runtime including candidate generation or clearly position CNV-Net as a downstream classifier. A fair benchmark must either integrate a standard candidate caller or explicitly separate the two stages.

- 201-bp window and shallow architecture lack biological context: CNVs span from 50 bases to millions of bases, yet CNV-Net processes only 201-base windows centered on breakpoints. The CNN uses four 3x3 convolutional layers with average pooling, yielding a limited receptive field that cannot capture the broader coverage shifts or flanking sequence context typical of structural variants. This narrow view forces the network to rely on local artifacts rather than genuine copy number signals. The paper provides no ablation study on window size or receptive field depth to justify this design choice. Expanding the input context or incorporating a dilated convolution strategy would better align the architecture with the biological scale of CNVs.

- Aggregate metrics mask critical size-dependent performance gaps: The reported F1 scores in Tables 1 and 2 are computed across all CNV sizes without stratification. Short-read sequencing struggles disproportionately with small CNVs and highly repetitive regions, yet the paper treats all sizes equally. Without size-stratified precision, recall, and breakpoint accuracy, readers cannot assess whether the model performs well on clinically relevant variants or merely on large, obvious deletions. The authors should decompose performance by CNV size bins and report breakpoint deviation metrics. This breakdown is essential to validate the claim of superior accuracy across the full CNV spectrum.

- Lack of bias correction undermines feature reliability: The pileup image generation pipeline encodes raw coverage and base qualities but omits any discussion of GC bias correction, mappability filtering, or handling of low-complexity regions. These systematic sequencing artifacts directly mimic copy number changes and are well-known confounders in CNV detection. If uncorrected, the CNN will likely learn to flag GC-rich or unmappable regions as breakpoints rather than true structural variants. The methodology section should detail how these biases are normalized or excluded before image encoding. Adding a bias correction step or at least a sensitivity analysis on known artifact-prone regions would strengthen the biological validity of the learned features.

- Limited evaluation scope restricts claims of broad utility: The model trains on NA12878 and tests on HG002, both sequenced at high depth with similar Illumina protocols. The authors claim applicability to other species and diverse cohorts but provide no cross-platform or cross-coverage validation. Performance on lower-coverage data, different read lengths, or non-human genomes remains untested. The literature also suggests several recent deep learning structural variant callers that the paper does not engage with, weakening the first-CNN claim. The authors should benchmark against recent deep learning baselines and include at least one evaluation on varied coverage depths or a different sequencing platform to support generalization claims.

- Missing post-processing pipeline for CNV calling: The paper frames CNV-Net as a complete CNV detection tool, yet it only outputs breakpoint probabilities in 201-bp windows. It never explains how these isolated predictions are aggregated into full CNV calls. Standard structural variant pipelines merge nearby breakpoint signals, apply coverage thresholds, and filter by size. Without this step, the reported F1 scores measure breakpoint classification accuracy rather than true CNV detection. Readers cannot assess the actual utility of the method without knowing how breakpoint probabilities translate into variant calls. The authors must detail the merging logic, distance thresholds, and size filters used downstream. Providing this pipeline specification is essential to validate the performance claims.

- Lack of size-stratified performance evaluation: CNVs span from 50 bases to millions of bases, and short-read sequencing exhibits well-known size-dependent detection limits. Reporting a single aggregate F1 score masks whether the model succeeds on clinically relevant small variants or only on large, obvious deletions. The paper claims superior accuracy across the CNV spectrum but provides no breakdown by variant length. Standard genomic benchmarking requires stratifying precision, recall, and breakpoint deviation into size bins. The authors should decompose the HG002 results into categories such as <1kb, 1-10kb, and >10kb. This stratification is necessary to prove the method actually generalizes across the biologically relevant size range.

- Unfair computational benchmark conflating inference with full detection: The runtime comparison in Table 3 measures only the CNN inference step for CNV-Net, while the competing tools perform candidate generation, statistical modeling, and CNV calling. The authors explicitly acknowledge that CNV-Net requires pre-identified candidate positions as input. Measuring only the classification stage artificially inflates the speed advantage. To deliver on the fast claim, the paper must either report the total pipeline runtime including a standard candidate caller or explicitly position CNV-Net as a downstream filter. A fair benchmark requires matching the input requirements and full workflow of all compared methods.

- Missing ablation on image encoding and bias handling: The pileup image generation encodes raw coverage and base qualities without discussing GC bias correction or mappability filtering. These systematic sequencing artifacts directly mimic copy number changes and are well-documented confounders in CNV detection. If uncorrected, the network likely learns to flag GC-rich or low-mappability regions as breakpoints rather than genuine structural variants. The methodology section omits any normalization step or sensitivity analysis for these known artifacts. The authors should include an ablation study showing performance with and without bias normalization, or at least report results restricted to high-mappability regions. This verification is required to confirm the network learns true biological signals.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Replace the sample-level train/val/test split with independent genomes to eliminate data leakage and re-evaluate Table 1.

- Report full-pipeline runtime including candidate generation, or reframe CNV-Net explicitly as a classifier and compare only against other breakpoint classifiers.

- Add size-stratified performance metrics and breakpoint precision analysis to Tables 1 and 2.

- Include a bias correction step in the image generation pipeline or provide a sensitivity analysis on GC content and mappability.

- Benchmark against recent deep learning structural variant callers and test generalization on at least one lower-coverage dataset or different sequencing platform.

comments.title: Arithmetic mismatch in CNV-Net F1-score
comments.explanation: The F1-score for CNV-Net in Table 2 does not match the precision and recall values in the same row. The harmonic mean of 0.7960 and 0.6596 is approximately 0.7216, not 0.6912. All other tools in the table compute F1 correctly from their listed precision and recall. This discrepancy suggests a calculation error or an undocumented metric definition. Correct the F1 value to 0.7216 or explain the alternative formula used.

comments.title: Early stopping criterion relies on training accuracy
comments.quote: To reduce overfitting, we train the model until the accuracy cannot be improved in 15 epochs.
comments.explanation: Monitoring training accuracy to trigger early stopping is methodologically unsound. Training accuracy typically rises or plateaus monotonically and does not reflect generalization performance. Early stopping should track validation loss or validation accuracy to halt training when the model begins to overfit. Replace the training accuracy criterion with a validation metric to ensure the reported performance reflects true generalization.

comments.title: Incorrect claim of data reconstructibility
comments.quote: 201 \times 55$  pixel pileup image, thus, captures coverage and reads specific to the 201-base region of the reference genome such that the sequencing data may be re-created from a series of consecutive pileup images.
comments.explanation: This statement is inaccurate. The encoding process is inherently lossy. The method caps reads at 45 per position, discards read start/end coordinates, read lengths, and paired-end information, and clips base qualities to a 0–255 range. None of these discarded elements are recoverable from the fixed-dimension image. Remove the clause about reconstructing sequencing data, as the original BAM file cannot be regenerated from the pileup images.

comments.title: Misclassification of Clairvoyante in novelty claim
comments.quote: Although it has been shown that CNNs such as DeepVariant (Poplin et al., 2018) and Clairvoyante (Luo et al., 2019) can accurately detect single nucleotide variants from similarly encoded images or tensors, few CNNs have been designed for the identification of CNVs.
comments.explanation: The text groups Clairvoyante with DeepVariant as an SNV caller, but Clairvoyante is explicitly a deep learning method for detecting structural variants and CNVs from single-molecule sequencing. Citing it as an SNV tool while claiming few CNNs exist for CNVs creates a direct contradiction and misrepresents the literature. Either remove Clairvoyante from the SNV example or acknowledge it as a direct predecessor in CNN-based CNV detection to accurately position the novelty of CNV-Net.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing regarding read nucleotide encoding
comments.quote: The R channel encodes for the nucleotide, which is the same as the reference genome, and the G channel encodes for the base quality found in the BAM file, which is adjusted to be between 0 and 255.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'which is the same as the reference genome' incorrectly implies that read bases are overwritten with reference bases, which would erase all mismatch information. The authors likely mean that the encoding scheme (A=250, G=180, etc.) matches the reference rows. Clarify that the encoding values are identical to the reference, not the nucleotide identities themselves, to prevent confusion about how mismatches are preserved.

comments.title: Contradictory data splitting description
comments.quote:
CNVs. From the NA12878 dataset, a total of 52,243 pileup images are labeled as duplications, deletions, and false positives from the training set randomly split into  $70\%$ ,  $20\%$ ,  $10\%$  categories for training set, validation set, and test set for the network, respectively.

#
comments.explanation: The sentence claims the images originate 'from the training set' but then describes splitting them into training, validation, and test categories. This is logically circular. The authors mean the full collection of 52,243 images is partitioned into these three sets. Rewrite the sentence to state that the complete dataset is divided into training (70%), validation (20%), and test (10%) splits, removing the confusing reference to the 'training set' as the source.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes CNV-Net, a CNN-based method for detecting CNV breakpoints from short-read WGS data encoded as RGB pileup images. The core idea of applying deep learning to CNV detection is timely, and the use of GIAB benchmark datasets is appropriate. However, the paper suffers from several critical flaws that undermine its claims: the evaluation is fundamentally flawed because CNV-Net is given candidate breakpoint positions while other methods must discover them from scratch, the training data labels are treated as gold standard without critical examination, and the method's reliance on candidate positions severely limits its practical utility. The paper also lacks essential details for reproducibility and does not address known confounders like GC bias.

Overview issues:

- Unfair and misleading comparison with existing tools: The paper states that CNV-Net makes predictions using pileup images of CNVs where their start and end positions are given, and hence we assume that candidate CNV positions are given as input. In contrast, other methods must discover candidate CNV positions from the sequencing data themselves. This is a fundamental asymmetry in the evaluation. CNV-Net is essentially a classifier that validates or refines pre-specified candidate breakpoints, not a de novo CNV caller. The comparison in Table 2 is therefore misleading: CNV-Net's task is much easier (binary classification on known candidate regions) than the other tools' task (full discovery). The paper should either (a) provide CNV-Net with the same candidate positions as the other tools discover, or (b) compare CNV-Net against a comparable validation/refinement tool rather than against full callers. Without this, the claimed superiority is not supported.

- Critical reliance on pre-specified candidate breakpoints without addressing how to obtain them: The paper acknowledges that CNV-Net needs candidate CNV positions as input, and suggests using outputs from other callers or developing a sensitive candidate detection algorithm. However, this is not a minor limitation—it is a fundamental architectural dependency. The paper never evaluates how the quality of candidate positions affects CNV-Net's performance. If candidate positions are derived from another caller, then CNV-Net's accuracy is upper-bounded by that caller's recall. Moreover, the paper does not provide any analysis of how many true CNVs are missed because they were not in the candidate set, nor does it evaluate the impact of false candidate positions on precision. Without this analysis, the practical utility of CNV-Net is unclear. The paper should include an end-to-end evaluation where candidate positions are generated by an existing caller, and report the combined recall/precision.

- Ground truth labels are treated as gold standard without validation: The paper uses NA12878 and HG002 benchmark calls from GIAB as ground truth. However, GIAB benchmarks are known to be incomplete and contain errors, especially for complex CNVs, small events, and events in repetitive regions. The paper does not discuss this limitation or attempt to validate the labels. For example, the NA12878 dataset contains 22,936 duplications and 18,614 deletions—these numbers are unusually high compared to typical estimates of ~1,000-2,000 CNVs per genome. This suggests that the benchmark may include many false positives or that the definition of CNV breakpoints is extremely permissive. The paper should discuss the quality of the ground truth, report how many calls were filtered, and ideally validate a subset using orthogonal methods or manual inspection.

- No correction for GC bias, mappability, or other systematic depth variations: The paper uses raw read depth as a feature without any correction for GC bias, mappability, or other known confounders. These factors cause systematic variation in read depth that can mimic CNV signals, especially in repetitive regions. The literature context explicitly warns about this. The paper does not describe any normalization or correction step beyond adjusting read depth values to 0-255. This is a serious omission because the CNN could learn to associate GC content or mappability patterns with CNV calls, leading to overfitting to the training dataset's specific biases. The paper should either (a) apply standard GC correction and mappability filtering before generating pileup images, or (b) demonstrate that the CNN is robust to these confounders by testing on datasets with different GC profiles.

- Severe class imbalance and small CNV detection not addressed: The training set contains 22,936 duplications, 18,614 deletions, and only 10,693 false positives. This is a highly imbalanced dataset where false positives (which should represent the vast majority of the genome) are severely underrepresented. The paper uses sparse categorical cross-entropy loss, which is not designed for class imbalance. No mention is made of class weighting, oversampling, or other techniques. Furthermore, the paper does not report performance stratified by CNV size. Given that small CNVs are both more numerous and harder to detect, the reported aggregate F1-scores may mask poor performance on clinically relevant small events. The paper should report precision/recall by CNV size bins and describe how class imbalance was handled.

- Insufficient detail for reproducibility of pileup image generation: The description of pileup image generation in Section 2.1 lacks critical details. The paper states that coverage rows are encoded with adjusted read depth values between 0 and 255, but does not specify the normalization formula. It also states that if more than 45 reads map to a region, those reads are discarded—this is a hard truncation that loses information in high-coverage regions (the HG002 dataset has 60x coverage). The paper does not specify how reads are selected when there are more than 45 (random? by mapping quality?). The base quality encoding is described as adjusted to be between 0 and 255, but the adjustment method is not given. These omissions make it impossible to reproduce the method. The paper should provide exact formulas for all normalization steps and the read selection procedure.

- No evaluation of breakpoint accuracy: The paper only reports event-level precision, recall, and F1-score. For CNV detection, breakpoint accuracy is equally important—a method that correctly identifies a deletion but predicts boundaries 500 bp away is less useful. The paper does not report any breakpoint deviation metrics (e.g., median absolute distance between predicted and true breakpoints, or proportion of calls within 100 bp/1 kb of true boundaries). Given that the method is designed to classify 201-base windows centered on breakpoints, it should be straightforward to assess how often the predicted breakpoint falls within the true window. The paper should include breakpoint accuracy statistics.

- Computational efficiency comparison is misleading: Table 3 reports that CNV-Net runs in 16.62 seconds on HG002, compared to 36-565 minutes for other tools. However, this comparison is unfair because CNV-Net is only classifying pre-specified candidate positions, not performing the full discovery pipeline. The time to generate candidate positions (which must come from another tool or from the GIAB benchmark) is not included. Moreover, the paper does not report training time, which for a CNN with 5 million parameters could be substantial (hours to days). The paper should report end-to-end runtime including candidate generation, and should also report training time and hardware specifications.

- No end-to-end evaluation with candidate generation: The paper acknowledges that CNV-Net requires pre-specified candidate breakpoints, but never evaluates the full pipeline where candidates come from an existing caller. Without this, the reported F1-score of 0.6912 on HG002 is not a fair comparison to other tools that perform de novo discovery. The paper should add an experiment where candidate positions are generated by a high-recall caller (e.g., Manta or CNVnator), then passed to CNV-Net for classification. The combined recall, precision, and F1 should be reported alongside the other tools' results. This would show whether CNV-Net adds value in a realistic use case.

- Missing correction for GC bias and mappability: The paper uses raw read depth as a feature without any normalization for GC bias, mappability, or other systematic depth variations. These confounders are known to produce false CNV signals, especially in repetitive regions. The CNN could learn spurious associations between GC content and CNV labels, leading to overfitting to the training dataset. The paper should either apply standard GC correction (e.g., by dividing coverage by the genome-wide average for each GC bin) and filter low-mappability regions before generating pileup images, or demonstrate that the model is robust to these confounders by testing on a dataset with a different GC profile (e.g., a different chromosome or a non-human genome).

- No performance breakdown by CNV size: The paper reports aggregate F1-scores but does not stratify by CNV size. Small CNVs (e.g., 50–500 bp) are harder to detect and more clinically relevant than large ones. Without size-stratified results, the reported F1 may be driven by easy-to-detect large events. The paper should bin CNVs by size (e.g., 50–500 bp, 500 bp–5 kbp, 5–50 kbp, >50 kbp) and report precision, recall, and F1 for each bin. This would reveal whether the method works well across the full size range or only for large events.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide an end-to-end evaluation where candidate breakpoint positions are generated by an existing caller (e.g., CNVnator or Manta), and report combined precision/recall/F1 for the full pipeline, not just the CNN classification step.

- Address GC bias and mappability confounders by applying standard normalization before pileup generation, or demonstrate robustness through experiments on datasets with different GC profiles.

- Report performance stratified by CNV size (e.g., <1 kb, 1-10 kb, 10-100 kb, >100 kb) and describe how class imbalance was handled in training.

- Provide exact, reproducible details for all normalization steps in pileup image generation, including read depth scaling, base quality adjustment, and read selection when coverage exceeds 45 reads.

- Include breakpoint accuracy metrics (e.g., median deviation, proportion within 100 bp/1 kb) and discuss the quality and limitations of the GIAB ground truth labels.

comments.title: Unfair comparison due to asymmetric task difficulty
comments.quote: It is important to note that CNV-Net makes predictions using the pileup images of CNVs where their start and end positions are given, and hence we assume that candidate CNV positions are given as input to CNV-Net. However, other methods find the candidate CNV positions using the information on how reads are mapped to the reference genome and detect CNVs on those candidate CNV positions.
comments.explanation: The paper acknowledges this asymmetry but does not correct for it in the comparison. CNV-Net is a classifier on pre-specified breakpoints, while the other tools perform de novo discovery. The F1-scores in Table 2 are therefore not comparable. The paper should either (a) provide CNV-Net with the same candidate positions as the other tools discover, or (b) compare CNV-Net against a comparable validation/refinement tool. Without this, the claim that CNV-Net is 'more accurate' is unsupported. Rewrite the comparison to include an end-to-end evaluation where candidates come from an existing caller, and report combined recall/precision.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of CNV-Net as a discovery tool
comments.quote:
.

Here, we present CNV-Net, a new approach to identify CNVs from DNA sequencing data using a six-layer deep convolutional neural network (CNN). CNV-Net first encodes the reads and their

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

related information from DNA sequencing data such as the type of base, base coverage (the number of reads covering a position), and base quality of each position into the RGB channels of images. Then it uses a deep CNN to predict these images as deletions, duplications, or false positives (not a CNV) breakpoints. Note that a CNV is defined by two breakpoints, th
comments.explanation: The introduction describes CNV-Net as an approach to 'identify CNVs from DNA sequencing data,' which implies de novo discovery. However, the method actually classifies pre-specified candidate breakpoints into deletions, duplications, or false positives. It does not discover CNV positions on its own. This framing is misleading because it sets up an expectation that CNV-Net performs the same task as other callers (full discovery), when in reality it only validates or refines candidate breakpoints. The paper should explicitly state in the introduction that CNV-Net requires candidate breakpoints as input and is a classifier, not a de novo caller. Rewrite the quoted passage to say: 'Here, we present CNV-Net, a new approach to classify candidate CNV breakpoints from DNA sequencing data using a six-layer deep convolutional neural network (CNN).'

comments.title: Ground truth labels may contain many false positives
comments.quote: From the NA12878 image dataset (Pendleton et al., 2015), we generate images for 22,936 duplications, 18,614 deletions, and 10,693 false positives (not CNVs) breakpoints.
comments.explanation: The numbers 22,936 duplications and 18,614 deletions are unusually high. Typical estimates suggest ~1,000–2,000 CNVs per genome. This suggests the GIAB benchmark may include many false positives or uses an extremely permissive definition of breakpoints. The paper does not discuss this discrepancy or validate the labels. This matters because the model is trained on these labels, so its reported accuracy may be inflated if the ground truth is noisy. The paper should discuss the quality of the ground truth, report how many calls were filtered, and ideally validate a subset using orthogonal methods.

comments.title: No correction for GC bias or mappability
comments.quote: The NA12878 dataset is preprocessed and converted into RGB images as described in the Methods section.
comments.explanation: The paper uses raw read depth as a feature without any normalization for GC bias, mappability, or other systematic depth variations. These confounders are known to produce false CNV signals, especially in repetitive regions. The CNN could learn spurious associations between GC content and CNV labels, leading to overfitting to the training dataset. The paper should either apply standard GC correction (e.g., by dividing coverage by the genome-wide average for each GC bin) and filter low-mappability regions before generating pileup images, or demonstrate that the model is robust to these confounders by testing on a dataset with a different GC profile.

comments.title: Runtime comparison is misleading
comments.quote: CNV-Net is significantly faster than other methods (Table 3). That is because a fitted convolutional neural network is efficient in making predictions, however, it may be time-consuming during model training. Run time is effectively front-loaded into model training instead of a concurrent statistical analysis. Another reason for this can be that CNV-Net makes predictions on candidate CNVs so it does not need to spend time searching for candidate CNVs in DNA sequencing data as if other methods do.
comments.explanation: The runtime comparison is unfair because CNV-Net only classifies pre-specified candidate positions, not performing the full discovery pipeline. The time to generate candidate positions (which must come from another tool or from the GIAB benchmark) is not included. Moreover, the paper does not report training time, which for a CNN with 5 million parameters could be substantial (hours to days). The paper should report end-to-end runtime including candidate generation, and should also report training time and hardware specifications.

comments.title: False positive image selection needs justification
comments.quote: ve images include random 201-base regions outside of CNVs. From the NA12878 dataset, a total of 52,243 pileup images are labeled as duplications, deletions, and false positives from the training set randomly split into  $70\%$ ,  $20\%$ ,  $10\%$  categories for training set, validation set, and test set for the network, respectively
comments.explanation: The paper states false positive images are 'random 201-base regions outside of CNVs' but does not specify how these regions are selected. If drawn uniformly from the genome outside CNVs, most will be from normal diploid regions with no structural variation signal, making the false positive class trivially easy to distinguish from true CNV breakpoints. The model could learn to classify based on the presence of any signal rather than the specific CNV pattern, inflating accuracy. The paper should describe the selection process (e.g., matched in GC content, mappability, or distance from CNVs) and justify that the task is not artificially easy. Without this, reported performance may not reflect real-world conditions where false positives arise from regions that look like CNVs but are not.

comments.title: Class imbalance not addressed in loss function
comments.quote: In training CNV-net, we use a standard Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba, 2014) with a sparse categorical cross-entropy loss function. We use a cyclical learning rate initialized at 1e-5 and maximal 1e-2. To reduce overfitting, we train the model until the accuracy cannot be improved in 15 epochs.
comments.explanation: The training set contains 22,936 duplications, 18,614 deletions, and only 10,693 false positives, a highly imbalanced dataset where false positives are severely underrepresented. Sparse categorical cross-entropy loss does not account for class imbalance; it treats all classes equally. Without class weighting, oversampling, or a specialized loss function, the model may achieve high accuracy by simply predicting the majority class (duplication) for most inputs, while performing poorly on the minority false positive class. The paper does not mention any technique to address this imbalance. The authors should either (a) apply class weighting in the loss function, (b) use oversampling/undersampling, or (c) report precision and recall per class to demonstrate that the model is not biased toward the majority class.

comments.title: Breakpoint localization accuracy not evaluated
comments.quote: The model predicts the probability of deletion ("DEL"), duplication ("DUP"), and false positive ("FP") breakpoint presence in each input pileup image.
comments.explanation: The paper only reports event-level precision, recall, and F1-score, but does not evaluate breakpoint accuracy. For CNV detection, the precise location of breakpoints is clinically and biologically important. Since the model classifies 201-base windows centered on candidate breakpoints, it is natural to ask how often the true breakpoint falls within the predicted window. The paper should report metrics such as the median absolute distance between predicted and true breakpoints, or the proportion of calls where the predicted breakpoint is within 100 bp, 500 bp, or 1 kb of the true breakpoint. Without this, the practical utility of the method for precise CNV boundary detection is unclear.

comments.title: No performance breakdown by CNV size
comments.quote: As shown in Table 1, CNV-Net identifies duplications with F1-score of 0.7833, deletions with F1-score of 0.7473, and false positives with F1-score of 0.7124. This result shows that CNV-Net achieves high accuracy in the test set of the NA12878 dataset.
comments.explanation: The paper reports aggregate F1-scores but does not stratify by CNV size. Small CNVs (e.g., 50–500 bp) are harder to detect and more clinically relevant than large ones. Without size-stratified results, the reported F1 may be driven by easy-to-detect large events. The paper should bin CNVs by size (e.g., 50–500 bp, 500 bp–5 kbp, 5–50 kbp, >50 kbp) and report precision, recall, and F1 for each bin. This would reveal whether the method works well across the full size range or only for large events.

comments.title: No justification for 201-base window size
comments.quote: The 201-base regions are centered around the CNV breakpoints, that is, the starts and ends of CNVs in the training set capturing the 100 positions directly before and after each CNV breakpoint.
comments.explanation: The paper uses a fixed 201-base window centered on breakpoints but provides no justification for this specific size. The choice of window size affects the model's ability to capture relevant read-depth patterns: too small a window may miss the signal, while too large a window may include irrelevant noise. The paper should explain why 201 bases was chosen (e.g., based on typical CNV size, read length, or empirical tuning) and discuss how sensitive results are to this parameter.

comments.title: Overstated claim about learning features with higher complexity
comments.quote: The key advantage of CNV-Net over other methods is the application of deep learning, which allows learning features with higher complexity from DNA sequencing data.
comments.explanation: This claim is vague and not substantiated. The paper does not demonstrate that CNV-Net learns features of 'higher complexity' than existing methods. In fact, the literature context notes that for structural variant filtering, simple Random Forest models achieve F1-scores nearly matching complex deep learning models, raising questions about whether added CNN complexity is justified. The paper should either provide evidence that CNV-Net captures features that simpler models cannot, or temper this claim. Replace the quoted sentence with a specific, testable claim such as: 'CNV-Net uses a deep CNN to learn spatial patterns in read depth, base quality, and base type across 201-base windows, which may capture breakpoint signatures that are missed by statistical models relying on summary statistics alone.'

comments.title: Missing normalization formula for read depth
comments.quote: We encode DNA sequencing information into RGB images and train the convolutional neural network with these images.
comments.explanation: The abstract mentions encoding sequencing information into RGB images but does not specify the normalization formula used to map read depth values to the 0–255 range. Without this detail, the method cannot be reproduced. The paper should provide the exact formula (e.g., min-max scaling, percentile clipping) in the abstract or refer to a detailed methods section.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a CNN-based classifier for CNV breakpoints using RGB-encoded pileup images. While it demonstrates strong performance on the NA12878 dataset, the contribution is limited by its reliance on pre-specified candidate positions, leading to an unfair comparison with full CNV detection tools and a questionable evaluation on a very small subset of the HG002 dataset.

The work is technically sound regarding the CNN architecture and image encoding but fails to deliver a complete, end-to-end solution. The framing as a 'detection' method obscures the fact that it is a component of a larger pipeline, and the benchmarking protocol undermines the claims of superiority over existing tools.

Overview issues:

- The method is a breakpoint classifier, not a CNV detector: The paper claims to 'detect' CNVs, but the core contribution is a binary classifier for breakpoint images given pre-specified positions. The conclusion admits this limitation, but the introduction and abstract overstate the capability, presenting the work as a complete solution rather than a component of a larger pipeline. This framing misleads readers about the practical utility of the method, as a tool that requires pre-labeled candidate positions is not a standalone detector. To fix this, the authors must either integrate a candidate finder into the pipeline or explicitly redefine the contribution as a specialized classifier for validated breakpoints.

- Benchmarking protocol is methodologically unsound: The comparison between CNV-Net and existing tools like Manta and Delly is unfair because CNV-Net requires candidate positions as input, while the other tools perform end-to-end detection. Furthermore, the runtime comparison ignores the cost of generating these candidates; if a user must run a separate caller to obtain positions, the total analysis time is significantly longer than the 16.62 seconds reported for CNV-Net. This creates a misleading impression of efficiency that does not reflect the actual workflow required to use the proposed method. The authors should compare CNV-Net against a pipeline that uses it (e.g., CNV-Net + Manta) to provide a fair evaluation of total runtime.

- Loss of information via read truncation limits accuracy: The image encoding method discards any read exceeding 45 bases in the 'reads' rows, a limitation that removes raw sequence information from high-coverage regions. Since the HG002 dataset has 60x coverage, a significant portion of the data will be truncated, potentially degrading the model's ability to distinguish true signal from noise. This truncation is not mentioned as a limitation, yet it likely contributes to the lower performance on the HG002 dataset compared to NA12878. The authors should either remove the 45-read limit or provide a justification for why this truncation does not affect the model's discriminative power.

- Evaluation on HG002 relies on insufficient sample size: The performance metrics for duplications on the HG002 dataset are based on only 174 samples, a sample size too small to provide statistically reliable estimates of precision and recall. Reporting an F1-score of 0.78 for duplications on NA12878 (52,243 samples) and claiming similar or superior performance on a dataset with fewer than 200 duplications is statistically weak. The paper should either provide confidence intervals for these metrics or aggregate the results over more samples to validate the HG002 findings.

Recommendation: Major Revision. The paper claims to improve CNV detection but relies on pre-existing candidate positions, making it a component rather than a full tool. The benchmarking comparison against end-to-end callers is unfair, and the evaluation on a very small subset of the HG002 dataset is statistically weak.

Revision targets:

- Redefine the contribution as a breakpoint classifier, not a full detector.

- Compare CNV-Net against a pipeline that uses it (e.g., CNV-Net + Manta) rather than standalone tools.

- Address the read truncation limit or justify why it does not affect performance.

- Re-evaluate HG002 results with appropriate statistical confidence or more data.

comments.title: Abstract and Introduction Misrepresent Method Scope
comments.quote: The fitted convolutional neural network can then be used to predict CNVs from DNA sequencing data.
comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction present CNV-Net as a complete solution for CNV detection, but the method actually requires pre-specified candidate breakpoints. While the conclusion acknowledges this limitation, the framing in the abstract and introduction misleads readers about the practical utility of the work. The authors should clarify that CNV-Net is a classifier for validated breakpoints, not a standalone detector.

comments.title: Runtime Comparison is Misleading
comments.quote: hat CNV-Net makes predictions using the pileup images of CNVs where their start and end positions are given, and hence we assume that candidate CNV positions are given as input to CNV-Net. However, other methods find the candidate CNV positions using the information on how reads are mapped to the reference genome and detect CNVs on those candidate CN
comments.explanation: Comparing the runtime of CNV-Net to tools like Manta is methodologically unsound because CNV-Net requires pre-labeled candidate positions as input, whereas Manta performs end-to-end detection. The reported runtime for CNV-Net does not account for the time required to generate these candidates using a separate caller. A fairer evaluation would compare CNV-Net against a pipeline that uses it (e.g., CNV-Net + Manta) to determine total analysis time.

comments.title: Read Truncation Limits Accuracy
comments.quote: If there are more than 45 reads mapping to a specific region, those reads are discarded; we instead compensate by capturing this information through the rows that encodes coverage.
comments.explanation: The method discards any read exceeding 45 bases in the 'reads' rows, a limitation that removes raw sequence information from high-coverage regions. Since the HG002 dataset has 60x coverage, a significant portion of the data is truncated, potentially degrading the model's ability to distinguish true signal from noise. This truncation is not mentioned as a limitation, yet it likely contributes to the performance gap between NA12878 and HG002. The authors should either remove the 45-read limit or provide a justification for why this truncation does not affect the model's discriminative power.

comments.title: HG002 Sample Size is Statistically Weak
comments.quote: As we only consider CNVs passing the quality control filter, this dataset contains 174 duplications, 10,668 deletions, and 5,205 false positives breakpoints post-filtering.
comments.explanation: The performance metrics for duplications on the HG002 dataset are based on only 174 samples, a sample size too small to provide statistically reliable estimates of precision and recall. Reporting an F1-score of 0.78 for duplications on NA12878 (52,243 samples) and claiming similar or superior performance on a dataset with fewer than 200 duplications is statistically weak. The authors should either provide confidence intervals for these metrics or aggregate the results over more samples to validate the HG002 findings.
